visual_checks: Check if the page appears to be designed exclusively for one input type (e.g., touch-only or mouse-only)
Look for any messaging suggesting a specific input method is required
Check if all functionality works with both mouse/touch and keyboard
pass_conditions: Content does not restrict or prevent use of any available input modality
Users can switch freely between touch, mouse, keyboard, and other inputs
No JavaScript detection of input type that restricts other modalities
fail_conditions: Content restricts input to touch-only on devices that also support keyboard/mouse
JavaScript detects touch capability and disables mouse or keyboard handlers
Content uses pointer-events: none to block specific input types inappropriately
common_mistakes: Mobile-first designs that disable mouse events when touch is detected
Gesture-only controls with no keyboard/mouse alternative
Using pointer-events: none on interactive elements
plain_meaning: Web content must not block users from using any input modality their platform supports. If a user wants to switch between touch, mouse, keyboard, or stylus mid-task, the content must accommodate that, unless restriction is essential, required for security, or required to honor user settings.
scope_applies_to: Content that disables or blocks specific input modes
Applications that force one specific input type
Scripts that reject keyboard events in favor of touch or vice versa
Mixed-input workflows where a user may switch devices mid-task
scope_does_not_apply_to: Respecting explicit user-agent or OS input settings
Content that simply responds to whichever input a user chooses without blocking others
borderline_calibration: Only flag when content actively refuses or breaks a supported input modality.
Supporting touch AND mouse AND keyboard concurrently is the default pass state.
Do not flag essential or security-driven restrictions.
Most web pages pass this SC without specific effort.
